messages.content:
Quick check: Should I also turn this into a 1-slide executive summary deck? (yes/no)

Lecture summary — QEEG-guided neuromodulation at scale

Context and scale
- Quote: \"אנחנו מכניסים בערך לאלף מטופלים בחודש... אחת הגדולות בעולם\" (Be’er Sheva unit)
- Goal: Precision treatment by mapping symptoms to brain circuits and selecting targeted neuromodulation.

Clinical framework (4-step protocol)
- 1) Diagnostic anchor: clarify primary disorder driving the chief complaint (e.g., amotivation from schizophrenia vs depression).
- 2) Symptom clustering: identify “circuit-linked” target symptoms, not just syndromes.
- 3) Brain mapping: QEEG against normative databases to localize dysregulated bands/regions.
- 4) Neurocognitive testing: select domains and quantify deficits (critical in MCI; domain-level guidance).

What QEEG adds
- QEEG translates EEG into normative, color-mapped deviations; enables region/band-specific hypotheses.
- Enables matching symptom clusters to stimulation targets; supports before/after objective change.
- Quote: \"ה-QEEG שלנו מתחיל להיות תקין\" after treatment.

Evidence cited
- Pragmatic RCT-like insight: In ~180 MDD patients (BrainsWay team), L-DLPFC vs midline stimulation had similar average outcomes, but subgroups defined by symptoms responded differentially—supports phenotype/circuit matching.
- Biomarker alignment: In OCD tDCS, responders showed BDNF increase vs placebo stable—biochemical corroboration.

Case vignettes (protocol logic)
- Case 1: Classic OCD with frontal high-beta excess (red, hyperarousal). Intervention: tDCS with cathodal downregulation over hyperactive region; ~20 sessions; QEEG normalization + symptom gains; BDNF rise in responders.
- Case 2: Depression with “brain-off” complaint and low posterior alpha (cold/blue). Intervention: tACS at individual alpha frequency (~9–10 Hz) to upregulate thalamo-cortical rhythms; QEEG normalizes even if mood lags, guiding continued care.
- Case 3: OCD with co-existing ADHD pattern and latent depressive signature. Risk: cathodal-only OCD protocol may worsen depression. Solution: dual-montage (simultaneous cathode + anode at different sites) tuned to avoid mood worsening; resulted in OCD improvement without depressive exacerbation.

Operational takeaways
- Precision comes from integrating: diagnosis → symptom cluster → QEEG pattern → neurocognitive domain → stimulation montage/parameters.
- tDCS/tACS are practical, scalable, low-burden; QEEG provides objective pre/post metrics and treatment steering.
- Not “one target per disorder”; it’s symptom-and-circuit-first.

Risks, caveats, compliance
- Clinical: Mis-targeting may worsen comorbid domains (e.g., depression while treating OCD); require screen for latent mood signatures on QEEG and brief scales.
- Evidence base: Subgroup-based targeting is promising but still sparse; maintain IRB/ethics for protocol innovation; careful claims.
- Safety: Standard NIBS contraindications; document adverse events; device governance and technician training.
- Data: Safeguard neurodata under HIPAA/GDPR equivalents; clearly separate clinical vs research consents.

Product and business implications (for our stack)
- Decision support: Map symptom clusters and neurocognitive profiles to QEEG patterns and propose montage presets (tDCS/tACS) with parameters and safety checks.
- Objective outcomes: Automate pre/post QEEG change scores + cognitive domain deltas; track biomarkers where feasible (BDNF optional in research).
- Differentiation: Circuit-matched stimulation at scale (1k/month) with measurable normalization is a moat; partner with device vendors and academic units.
- ROI: Higher response rates via targeting; fewer wasted sessions; protocol libraries reduce clinician time; standardized QA.

Stakeholder-tailored notes
- Dekel (clinical/research): Subgroup targeting is consistent with circuit psychiatry; propose pragmatic registry with QEEG + symptom domains + montage + outcomes.
- Oren (ROI/compliance): Build CPT/99091-compatible workflows, device utilization metrics, SAEs log; align claims to evidence; guardrails in app.
- Adiel (differentiation/partnerships): Pilot with Be’er Sheva unit + device partner; publish QEEG-normalization as objective endpoint.
- Nadav (user insights): Patients resonate with “brain map” visuals; give simple progress visuals tied to symptom they care about (sleep, rumination, energy).

Options and trade-offs
- Option A: Launch QEEG-guided NIBS pilot in 1 clinic
  - Pros: Fast learning loop, controllable risk, concrete data; near-term revenue
  - Cons: Limited generalizability until scaled
- Option B: Partner with device company for multicenter study
  - Pros: Credibility, shared costs, faster recruitment; publication potential
  - Cons: Slower, shared IP, coordination overhead
- Option C: Software-only DSS (no NIBS delivery) licensing
  - Pros: Low regulatory footprint, scalable
  - Cons: Less outcome control; dependent on external clinics

Recommendation
- Pursue Option A now, architected to expand into B with a partner; build DSS features in parallel.

Immediate next steps (owner • due date)
- Define pilot protocol: inclusion/exclusion, symptom clusters, QEEG bands, tDCS/tACS presets, safety checklists • Dekel • +7 days
- Data model: QEEG features, symptom scales, cognitive domains, session logs, outcomes; IRB-lite registry plan • Nadav • +10 days
- Vendor shortlist and device SOPs; service economics and reimbursement mapping • Oren • +10 days
- Partnership outreach (BrainsWay/others); draft joint study one-pager • Adiel • +14 days
- Build clinician-facing decision support v0.1 (intake → map → montage suggestion → safety) • PM/Eng • +21 days

KPIs
- Clinical: ≥20% improvement in target symptom within 4 weeks; QEEG deviation reduction in targeted band/region.
- Ops: ≥85% protocol adherence; <3% session reschedules; SAE rate 0.
- Business: Session margin >30%; time-to-first-response <7 days from intake.

Want me to convert this into a 1-page for investors and a technician protocol checklist?
